Tech Conference 2026 · Architecture Track

Microservices
in the Wild

Patterns, pitfalls, and practical lessons from production systems

45 min talk · 12 slides · ← → to navigate
The Starting Point

Every system starts as a monolith

  • → Simple to deploy — one artifact, one process, one database. Works great until it doesn't.
  • ! As teams and codebases grow, coupling becomes the dominant cost: slow builds, risky deploys, scaling bottlenecks.
  • ✗ The pain point: you need to scale the checkout service, but you can only scale the entire application.
  • ? Microservices solve organizational and operational coupling — not complexity. Know the trade before you split.
Definition

What makes a service "micro"?

A microservice is an independently deployable unit of software that models a single, bounded domain capability.


single responsibility own data store independent deploy loose coupling failure isolation
service-manifest.yaml 
name: order-service
version: 2.4.1
owns:
  domain:  orders
  database: orders-pg
  topics:
    - orders.created
    - orders.fulfilled
consumes:
  - inventory.reserved
  - payments.confirmed
team:       commerce
slo:
  availability: 99.9
  p99_ms:      120
Service Communication

Sync vs. async — choose deliberately

Synchronous (REST / gRPC)

  • ✓ Simple request-response model
  • ✓ Strong typing with gRPC + Protobuf
  • ✓ Easy to trace and debug
  • ✗ Temporal coupling — caller blocks
  • ✗ Cascading failures if downstream is slow
  • ✗ Both services must be up simultaneously

Asynchronous (Kafka / AMQP)

  • ✓ Temporal decoupling — fire and forget
  • ✓ Natural backpressure and buffering
  • ✓ Survives downstream outages
  • ✗ Eventual consistency by default
  • ✗ Harder to trace distributed flows
  • ✗ Requires idempotent consumers
Rule of thumb: use sync for queries that need real-time answers; use async for state-changing commands that don't need an immediate result.
Data Ownership

Never share a database

  • ✗ Shared schemas create invisible coupling: any service can mutate your data.
  • ✓ Each service owns its schema — other services ask via API, not via SQL joins.
  • ! Cross-service reads: publish events or expose read-model APIs. Never a foreign key across service boundaries.
anti-pattern.sql 
-- ❌ WRONG: cross-service join
SELECT o.id, u.email
FROM   orders.orders o
JOIN   users.users u   -- ← different DB!
  ON   o.user_id = u.id;

-- ✅ RIGHT: call the User API
-- GET /users/{id}  → { email }
-- or subscribe to users.updated event
System Architecture

Typical production topology

Web App
React / Next.js
Mobile
iOS / Android
CLI
internal tooling
↓   HTTPS
API Gateway / BFF
auth, rate-limit, routing, observability
↓   gRPC / REST
user-svc
Postgres
·
order-svc
Postgres
·
inventory-svc
Postgres
·
notif-svc
Redis + SMTP
↑↓   events
Kafka / RabbitMQ
orders.created · inventory.reserved · payments.confirmed
Observability

You can't debug what you can't see

📋

Structured Logs

JSON logs with trace_id, span_id, and service fields. Ship to your log aggregator — never grep prod.

📈

Metrics

RED method: Rate, Errors, Duration. Instrument every service boundary. Prometheus + Grafana.

🔍

Distributed Tracing

Propagate W3C traceparent headers. Use OpenTelemetry — vendor-neutral. One trace per request, crosses all services.

🚨

Alerting on SLOs

Alert on error budget burn rate, not raw error %. Reduces alert fatigue dramatically.

The three pillars (logs, metrics, traces) are table stakes. The real game is correlating them: one click from a trace to its logs to the offending metric.
Resilience Patterns

Assume everything will fail

  • CB Circuit Breaker — trip after N failures, fast-fail while open, recover with half-open probe. Prevents cascading failures.
  • R Retry with exponential backoff + jitter — never retry immediately or in sync. Cap at 3 attempts. Add random jitter to avoid thundering herds.
  • T Timeout budgets — every outbound call must have an explicit timeout. Propagate remaining deadline via context (grpc-timeout / X-Timeout-Ms).
  • B Bulkhead — isolate thread pools or connection pools per downstream. One slow dependency can't starve others.
Deployment

Ship with confidence

  • K8 Kubernetes: Deployment + HPA + PodDisruptionBudget. Never run fewer than 2 replicas in prod.
  • BG Blue/green or canary deploys. Route 5% → 25% → 100% with automatic rollback on SLO breach.
  • FF Feature flags decouple deploy from release. Ship dark, enable gradually, kill switch always available.
canary-rollout.yaml 
apiVersion: flagger.app/v1beta1
kind:       Canary
spec:
  analysis:
    interval:   1m
    threshold:  5  # max failed checks
    maxWeight:  50 # cap canary at 50%
    stepWeight: 10 # 10% per step
    metrics:
      - name:      error-rate
        threshold: 1  # rollback if > 1%
      - name:      latency-p99
        threshold: 200 # ms
Hard-Won Lessons

Pitfalls that will burn you

  • 1 Distributed monolith — services that deploy together or share a database aren't microservices. They're a monolith with network latency.
  • 2 No service mesh → no security — mTLS between services is non-negotiable. Flat networks mean one breach = full lateral movement.
  • 3 Chatty services — if service A calls B calls C calls D for every request, you've built an RPC chain that multiplies latency and failure probability.
  • 4 Premature decomposition — splitting before you understand domain boundaries creates wrong cuts you'll pay for years. Start monolith, split along seams.
The Honest Slide

When you should NOT use microservices

The operational overhead of microservices is real. Containers, service mesh, distributed tracing, eventual consistency — these require platform engineering maturity to do safely.
  • ✗ Team smaller than ~10 engineers. Conway's Law works both ways — small teams shouldn't own 20 services.
  • ✗ Domain not yet understood. Wrong service boundaries are worse than no service boundaries.
  • ✗ No dedicated platform/infra function. Someone has to own the cluster, the mesh, the observability stack.
  • ✓ A well-structured modular monolith is a valid architecture — and often the right one for year 1.
Key Takeaways

Three things to remember

🏗️

Own your boundaries

Split by domain, not by size. A service boundary is a contract — get it wrong and you'll feel it forever.

🔭

Instrument everything

Logs + metrics + traces from day one. Retrofitting observability into prod is a nightmare.

🔥

Design for failure

Every remote call is a potential outage. Timeouts, retries, circuit breakers — pick all three.

Thank you · Questions?
